programs.name: Med-QUEST Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers
programs.tagline: Your parent can receive care at home instead of a nursing facility — Hawaii covers services like personal care, respite, and home modifications.

localEntities.name: Med-QUEST Division, Department of Human Services
Developmental Disabilities Division (DDD), Department of Health

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, Hawaii's HCBS Waivers can cover the services that make that possible. These waivers pay for personal care, respite care, home modifications, and other supports so your parent can avoid institutional care.

Because this program requires nursing home level of care qualification, your parent must need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management. The program has multiple waivers for different populations — seniors, people with developmental disabilities, and others — each with specific eligibility requirements beyond basic Medicaid.

Your parent must qualify for Medicaid first, then meet additional requirements for the specific waiver program. Asset limits apply: $2,000 for singles age 65+ seeking long-term care services.

summary: Needs nursing home level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet nursing home level of care (clinical assessment showing your parent needs daily assistance with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Eligible for specific division services (varies by waiver type)
Cannot safely remain in community without waiver services

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through My Medical Benefits or call 1-877-628-5076 — processing time not specified and waitlists likely exist.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid first
steps.description: Complete Med-QUEST application online at medquest.hawaii.gov or call 1-877-628-5076. You must qualify for Medicaid before accessing any HCBS waiver.

steps.title: Contact the appropriate division
steps.description: For developmental disabilities, contact DDD after Medicaid approval. For other waivers, Med-QUEST will guide you to the right program based on your parent's needs.

steps.title: Complete level of care assessment
steps.description: A clinical assessment determines if your parent meets nursing home level of care. This evaluates daily living activities and medical needs.

steps.title: Submit HCBS waiver application
steps.description: Complete the 1915(c) HCBS Waiver Application form with required medical and functional documentation.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Waitlists likely due to limited waiver slots
applicationGuide.tip: Start the Medicaid application first — you cannot access HCBS waivers without Medicaid eligibility.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (Age 65+)
included: Retirement accounts your parent can access

tiers.name: Developmental Disabilities Waiver
tiers.description: For adults with intellectual or developmental disabilities
tiers.coverage: Case management, residential supports, day services, respite care

tiers.name: Elderly/Disabled Waiver
tiers.description: For seniors or adults with disabilities needing nursing home level care
tiers.coverage: Personal care, home modifications, respite care, adult day health

contentSections.text: HCBS waivers have limited slots and waitlists are common. Apply as soon as your parent meets the eligibility requirements — earlier applications get priority.
Multiple eligibility layers required: Your parent must qualify for (1) Medicaid, (2) the specific division's services, AND (3) nursing home level of care. Start with Medicaid first.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for HCBS?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit, though equity limits may apply. Your parent can remain in their home — that's the whole point of these waivers.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Hawaii has different Medicaid pathways with varying income limits. Some allow up to 138% of federal poverty level. There may also be spend-down options where medical expenses can reduce countable income.

faqs.question: How long do we wait after applying?
faqs.answer: Processing times aren't specified, but expect delays. HCBS waivers have limited slots, so waitlists are common. Apply as early as possible once your parent qualifies.

faqs.question: Can we choose which services my parent receives?
faqs.answer: Yes, services are person-centered and based on your parent's assessed needs. You'll work with a care coordinator to develop an individual service plan.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent needs more care than the waiver covers?
faqs.answer: The waiver must provide enough services to keep your parent safely in the community. If needs exceed what's available through HCBS, institutional care might be necessary.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to use specific providers?
faqs.answer: Services come through approved providers or the Consumer-Directed Option, which gives your family more control over hiring and managing care workers.

programs.name: Hawai'i PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: Your parent gets all medical care, adult day services, and support through one team instead of coordinating multiple providers.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay in their community, Hawai'i PACE provides everything through one program. Your parent gets medical care, adult day programs, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and all support services coordinated by a single team.

Because PACE becomes your parent's sole Medicare and Medicaid provider, they cannot use other doctors or services for covered care. This means no more coordinating between specialists, no more managing multiple insurance claims, but also no choice in providers once enrolled.

PACE is only available in specific geographic areas of Hawaii, and your parent must qualify for Medicaid and meet Hawaii's specific care level requirements (acuity level A or C).

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as requiring nursing home level of care (acuity level A or C in Hawaii) — meaning your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility
otherRequirements: Must live within a PACE provider's service area
Cannot be enrolled in Hawaii QUEST program
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage or Medicare prescription drug plans
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly, but processing times are not publicly available.

steps.title: Find your local PACE provider
steps.description: Contact Hawaii Department of Human Services to identify PACE providers serving your parent's area, as provider locations are not publicly listed.

steps.title: Schedule assessment
steps.description: The PACE provider will assess whether your parent meets Hawaii's acuity level A or C requirements for nursing home level care.

steps.title: Sign enrollment statement
steps.description: If eligible, your parent signs a statement requesting PACE services, understanding that PACE becomes their sole provider for Medicare and Medicaid services.

applicationGuide.tip: Before applying, confirm your parent is not enrolled in Hawaii QUEST program, as this disqualifies them from PACE.

contentSections.text: PACE becomes your parent's exclusive provider for all Medicare and Medicaid services. They cannot see other doctors or specialists outside the PACE network for covered care.
Your parent can leave PACE at any time, but re-enrollment may have different requirements. Consider this carefully before enrolling.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctor if they enroll in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE becomes your parent's sole provider for all Medicare and Medicaid covered services. They must use PACE doctors, specialists, and facilities exclusively.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Hawaii's acuity levels A and C?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify what acuity levels A and C mean in Hawaii's system. Contact the Hawaii Department of Human Services for details on these specific care level requirements.

faqs.question: Can my parent be in PACE if they're already on QUEST?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent cannot be enrolled in Hawaii's QUEST program (Hawaii's Medicaid managed care) and PACE at the same time.

faqs.question: What if there's no PACE provider in my parent's area?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot enroll in PACE. The program is only available in specific geographic service areas where PACE providers operate.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent moves to a different part of Hawaii?
faqs.answer: If they move outside their PACE provider's service area, they would need to disenroll from PACE and find alternative care arrangements.

faqs.question: Are there income limits for PACE in Hawaii?
faqs.answer: The Hawaii regulations don't specify income limits. Since PACE requires Medicaid eligibility, your parent would need to meet Medicaid income requirements, but specific amounts aren't published.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Hawaii helps pay your parent's Medicare premiums and costs — with income limits 15% higher than most states.
programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare premiums, deductibles, or copays, Hawaii's Medicare Savings Programs can help. These three programs (QMB, SLMB, and QI) pay different levels of Medicare costs based on your parent's income — from covering just the $202.90 monthly Part B premium to paying all Medicare deductibles and copayments.

Hawaii offers a significant advantage: income limits are approximately 15% higher than federal standards. A single person can qualify with monthly income up to $1,816 (QI program), compared to lower limits in most other states. Because Hawaii recognizes its higher cost of living, your parent may qualify even if they wouldn't elsewhere.

All three programs also make your parent automatically eligible for Extra Help with prescription drug costs (except QI), which caps drug copays at $12.65 per prescription in 2026.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premium savings alone ($202.90/month × 12 months = $2,435/year), with QMB providing additional deductible and copayment coverage

summary: Must have Medicare Part A and B
otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A and Part B
Must be U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Cannot qualify for QI if already on Medicaid

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through Hawaii MedQuest by phone, mail, or in-person — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Contact Hawaii MedQuest
steps.description: Call MedQuest or visit a local office to request Medicare Savings Program application forms.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, Hawaii residency, citizenship status, and Medicare enrollment.

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Return application with all supporting documents by phone, mail, or in-person.

steps.title: For QI program only
steps.description: Apply early in the year — QI operates first-come, first-served and requires annual reapplication.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program operates first-come, first-served and requires annual reapplication. QMB and SLMB have no waitlist.
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has any working income, mention this during application — Hawaii may allow higher income limits for earned income.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Program Type (2026)

rows.covers: Part B premium only, first-come first-served

contentSections.footnote: Hawaii income limits are 15% higher than federal standards. Add $500/month for each additional legal dependent.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Specific exempt assets not detailed in available information — contact MedQuest directly for complete list

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualified immigrant status

categories.name: Financial
items: Recent pay stubs or benefit statements

contentSections.text: QI program operates first-come, first-served each year and requires annual reapplication. Apply early in the calendar year to secure a spot.
Hawaii applies a $20 monthly income disregard to all programs, meaning your parent may qualify even if their income is slightly over the stated limits.

faqs.question: What's the difference between QMB, SLMB, and QI?
faqs.answer: QMB covers the most — all Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments. SLMB and QI only cover the Part B premium ($202.90/month in 2026). QI has the highest income limits but operates first-come, first-served and requires yearly reapplication.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify which assets are exempt, but typically primary residences don't count toward asset limits. Contact Hawaii MedQuest directly to confirm what assets are excluded from the $9,950 individual limit.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Hawaii applies a $20 monthly income disregard, so he may still qualify. If he has any working income, different rules may apply that allow higher income limits. Also, Hawaii's limits are 15% higher than most states.

faqs.question: Does this affect Mom's other Medicare benefits?
faqs.answer: No, this enhances Medicare by paying costs Medicare doesn't cover. QMB and SLMB also automatically qualify her for Extra Help with prescription drugs, capping copays at $12.65 per drug in 2026.

faqs.question: How long does approval take?
faqs.answer: Processing times aren't specified in available information. Contact Hawaii MedQuest directly for current timelines. QMB and SLMB don't require annual reapplication once approved, but QI does.

faqs.question: What if Dad already has Medicaid?
faqs.answer: He cannot qualify for the QI program if he already has Medicaid, but he may still be eligible for QMB or SLMB, which provide more comprehensive Medicare cost coverage anyway.
